comments.text: This reminds me of the ethos behind early Lisp Machine companies and later, the Symbolics approach to vertical integration. The insight that "I like computers and want to make them better" is actually a stronger founding motivation than chasing a market or solving a problem for others. Feels refreshing to

kids.text: This is a refreshing read but I wish he'd engaged more directly with the actual technical choices exe.dev is making rather than just the philosophical framing. The motivation is fine, but what does vertical integration look like here concretely? What's different from existing bare-metal cloud providers?

> The motivation is fine, but what does vertical integration look like here concretely?

I think you're asking the wrong question. Vertical integration isn't the goal here-it's a means. The real reframe is: instead of asking "what technical choices differentiate exe.dev," ask "what does it mean to build infrastructure from first principles when you actually like the medium itself?"

Most cloud founders are solving for a market gap or a cost problem. Crawshaw seems to be solving for coherence-the ability to make systems that reflect a unified vision, top to bottom. That's a very

I appreciate the sentiment, but "I like computers" doesn't actually explain why this needs to be a venture-backed company. Plenty of people like computers and just build things without raising capital. What's the funding going toward that couldn't be done bootstrapped or through a smaller team?

comments.text: Have you looked at Nix and NixOS? It sounds like you're trying to solve some of the same problems around declarative, reproducible infrastructure that Eelco Dolstra started with. https://nixos.org might save you a lot of reinvention.

kids.text:
Nix is interesting but operates at a different layer than what he's describing. NixOS solves reproducibility for a single machine or fleet you control; exe.dev seems to be about building cloud infrastructure that users deploy to, which requires thinking about networking, storage orchestration, multi-tenancy, billing, and all the operational complexity that Nix sidesteps by assuming you own the hardware.

That said, the declarative config idea is definitely borrowed from that tradition. Worth reading the Nix papers if you haven't; the core insight about content addressing and immutability shows up everywhere now (Bazel, Earthly, even some container runtimes). But building a cloud product means solving a much larger surface area of problems.

I actually used NixOS for about three years while building distributed systems at a previous company. The declarative approach is elegant in theory, but we hit some real friction points around non-determinism in the supply chain (firmware updates, kernel patches, binary caches going stale). For what Crawford is describing with

If you're hitting supply chain non-determinism issues, you might want to look at what the Unikraft project is doing. They're building unikernels with reproducible builds baked in from the start, so you get a single immutable artifact instead of trying to patch and layer on top of existing distros. No package manager, no stale caches, no firmware surprises.

It's a different philosophy than NixOS (minimal vs declarative), but for the "I just want a known-good image in production" use case it sidesteps a lot of the problems you ran into. Worth a look if exe.dev is targeting determinism across bare metal deployments.

comments.text: This reads like the kind of earnest founder manifesto that gets a lot of upvotes on HN, but I wonder if we're going to see the same pattern repeat: someone who's already won the game once deciding to build infrastructure because they "like computers," then discovering that boring, thankless infrastructure businesses

kids.text:
The pattern you're describing is real, but I think the framing misses something important about infrastructure companies that actually succeed.

The ones that work aren't built by people who view infrastructure as a stepping stone or a philosophical exercise. They're built by people who are genuinely frustrated with the existing options because they use them every day. Bezos didn't start AWS because he liked computers in the abstract-he started it because Amazon's infrastructure was a bottleneck and he saw an arbitrage opportunity. Torvalds didn't build Linux because he wanted to build infrastructure; he wanted a Unix-like system he could actually use and modify.

The "I like computers" framing might sound naive on the surface, but it's actually the opposite of the problem you're identifying. Someone who's already cashed out and is now chasing prestige or challenge typically fails at infrastructure because they lack the patience for the unsexy work. They want to build the novel part and delegate the boring part.

What matters is whether Craw

I'd push back on the nostalgia here. This reads like the "I just love building things" narrative that worked for infrastructure founders in 2010-2015, but the economics have fundamentally changed. AWS won. The regulatory environment is getting tighter (see the FTC's infrastructure scrutiny, EU data residency rules). And the market for greenfield cloud platforms is... what exactly? Who's switching from AWS because someone "likes computers"?

The pattern that worries me is founders mistaking personal enjoyment for

AWS also won on compute, but Lambda has real constraints that Lambda-adjacent platforms can exploit. Same with storage, networking, observability. The economics changed, but not uniformly.

The actual risk isn't "who switches from AWS" (almost nobody). It's "who never tries AWS in the first place because egress costs, vendor lock-in, or latency make it unusable for their workload?" See: Fly.io, Railway. They're
